Pei Chun Public School — P6 Science WA2 2025

Primary 6 · Term 2 Weighted Assessment 2025 · Science (Booklets A & B). Total: 60 marks. With answer key and explanations.

Booklet A — Multiple Choice (Questions 1–16, 2 marks each)

Question 1

2 marks

The table below shows three characteristics, A, B and C, of some animals. A tick (✓) shows that the characteristic is present.

AnimalABC
eagle✓✓✓
snake✓
butterfly✓✓

What do A, B and C represent?

ABC
(1)has wingshas scaleshas legs
(2)has wingslays eggshas scales
(3)has feathershas wingshas legs
(4)has featherslays eggshas wings

Answer

Option 4. All three animals lay eggs (characteristic B is present in all three), so B = lays eggs. Only the eagle has feathers (A), and the eagle and butterfly both have wings (C). Option 4 fits: A = has feathers (eagle only), B = lays eggs (all three), C = has wings (eagle and butterfly).

Question 2

2 marks

Which of the following is not a form of response to changes?

  1. A child grew taller over a year.
  2. A snail hid in its shell when touched.
  3. A pufferfish inflated its body when threatened.
  4. A frog jumped into a pond when a snake approached.

Answer

Option 1. Growing taller is part of growth, not a response to a stimulus. The other three describe an organism reacting to a change in its surroundings (touch, threat, an approaching predator), which are responses to stimuli.

Question 3

2 marks

Three animals are classified in the flowchart shown below.

What are questions P and Q?

Question PQuestion Q
(1)Does it have a 3-stage life cycle?Does the adult have wings?
(2)Does it have a 4-stage life cycle?Does the adult have wings?
(3)Does the young look like the adult?Does it have a 3-stage life cycle?
(4)Does the young look like the adult?Does it have a 4-stage life cycle?

Answer

Option 4. Question P separates the cockroach (whose young, the nymph, looks like the adult – "Yes") from the others, so P = "Does the young look like the adult?". Question Q then separates the mealworm beetle, which has a 4-stage (complete) life cycle – egg, larva, pupa, adult ("Yes") – from the frog, so Q = "Does it have a 4-stage life cycle?".

Question 4

2 marks

The diagram below shows the life cycle of a flowering plant.

Which of the following is correct?

Process(es) at MProcess(es) at N
(1)fertilisationpollination
(2)pollination and fertilisationdispersal and germination
(3)dispersalpollination and germination
(4)pollination and germinationfertilisation and dispersal

Answer

Option 2. M is the stage from adult plant to seed: the adult plant flowers, undergoes pollination and then fertilisation to form seeds. N is the stage from seed to young plant: the seed is first dispersed and then germinates to grow into a young plant. So M = pollination and fertilisation, N = dispersal and germination.

Question 5

2 marks

The diagram below shows two cells, P and Q.

Which of the following is correct?

Plant cellFunction of E
(1)Pcontrols movement of substances in and out of the cell
(2)Pgives the cell a shape
(3)Qcontrols movement of substances in and out of the cell
(4)Qgives the cell a shape

Answer

Option 3. Cell Q has a regular (rectangular) shape, a large central vacuole and chloroplasts, so Q is the plant cell. Part E is the cell membrane, which controls the movement of substances into and out of the cell. (Giving the cell a fixed shape is the job of the cell wall, not E.)

Question 6

2 marks

An experiment was set up as shown below.

What would happen to the drop of red ink after a while and what was the reason?

Direction the drop of red ink movedReason
(1)←The germinating seeds took in oxygen.
(2)→The germinating seeds gave out oxygen.
(3)←The germinating seeds took in carbon dioxide.
(4)→The germinating seeds gave out carbon dioxide.

Answer

Option 1. The germinating seeds respire, taking in oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide. The carbon dioxide is absorbed by the substance, so the total amount of gas in the tube falls. This lowers the air pressure inside, so the drop of red ink moves inwards (←, towards the seeds).

Question 7

2 marks

A student made three statements about sexual reproduction in plants and animals.

  • A The fertilised egg is found in the ovary.
  • B Reproductive cells are produced in the anthers.
  • C Fertilisation occurs in the female reproductive part.

Which of the following is correct?

PlantsHumans
(1)BA, C
(2)A, CA
(3)A, B, CA, C
(4)A, B, CC

Answer

Option 4. In plants: the fertilised egg (seed) forms in the ovary (A true), pollen is made in the anthers (B true), and fertilisation happens in the ovary, the female part (C true) – so A, B, C. In humans: there are no anthers and the fertilised egg develops in the womb, not an ovary, but fertilisation does occur in the female reproductive part (oviduct) – so only C.

Question 8

2 marks

The diagram below shows a pond habitat with some living things.

Based on the diagram above, how many populations of producers and consumers can be found in this habitat?

ProducersConsumers
(1)threethree
(2)threefour
(3)fourthree
(4)eleveneleven

Answer

Option 1. A population is all the organisms of one kind. The producers (plants that make their own food) are the water lily, the reeds on the bank and the underwater water-plants – three populations. The consumers are the turtles, the frog, the fish and the tadpoles – but the tadpoles are the young of the frog, so frog and tadpole are one population, giving turtle, frog and fish = three populations of consumers.

Question 9

2 marks

The diagram below shows a food web in a habitat.

A large number of organism E was introduced into the habitat. E only feeds on one type of organism in this habitat. After some time, all the other organisms decreased in number.

Which organism did E feed on?

  1. A
  2. B
  3. C
  4. D

Answer

Option 2. The arrows show that B is eaten by A, and A in turn supports C and D. So B is at the base of this web. If E eats B, the number of B falls, which reduces A, and then C and D fall too – causing all the other organisms to decrease. Feeding on any other organism would not affect every one of the others.

Question 10

2 marks

The diagram shows an insect with several adaptations which help to protect it from predators.

  • A It spreads its forelegs to appear bigger.
  • B It has feelers which help it to detect danger.
  • C It fans its wings to appear more threatening.

Which of the adaptations are structural adaptations?

  1. A only
  2. B only
  3. A and C only
  4. A, B and C

Answer

Option 2. A structural adaptation is a body part the animal has, not something it does. Spreading the forelegs (A) and fanning the wings (C) are actions, so they are behavioural adaptations. Only B – having feelers (a body structure) – is a structural adaptation.

Question 11

2 marks

The diagram shows a sealed container which holds 60 cm³ of water and 40 cm³ of air as shown below. 30 cm³ of water was removed from the container through the tap and 20 cm³ of air was then pumped in using the pump.

What would be the final volume of air in the container?

  1. 20 cm³
  2. 60 cm³
  3. 70 cm³
  4. 80 cm³

Answer

Option 3. The container is sealed, so its total volume stays 100 cm³. Removing 30 cm³ of water leaves 30 cm³ of water, so air now fills 100 − 30 = 70 cm³. Pumping in 20 cm³ more air does not change the space available – air still occupies all the space not taken by water, which is 70 cm³.

Question 12

2 marks

Alex looks into a fish tank as shown.

Which statement explains why he can see the glass?

  1. Light is reflected from the fish.
  2. No light passes through the fish.
  3. Light passes through the glass easily.
  4. Some light is reflected from the glass.

Answer

Option 4. We see an object when light from it enters our eyes. Even though glass is transparent, some light bounces (is reflected) off its surface and travels to Alex's eyes, allowing him to see the glass itself.

Question 13

2 marks

Jamus attached 5 identical drops of wax on an oval-shaped metal wire. The metal wire was heated by a heat source for five minutes.

Which of the following shows the order that the drops of wax would drop off the metal wire?

  1. A → E → D → C → B
  2. A → B → E → C → D
  3. B → C → D → E → A
  4. B → A → E → C → D

Answer

Option 2. Heat travels by conduction along the wire, away from the heat source which is at A. The drop nearest the heat (A) gets hot and falls first; the heat then spreads in both directions around the loop, reaching the drops in order of distance from A: B and E (next), then C, then D last. This matches A → B → E → C → D.

Question 14

2 marks

Shriya had two beakers of water of different volumes but at the same temperature as shown below. She added all the water from beaker Y into beaker X.

The volume of water in beaker X increased to 300 ml. She measured the temperature of the water immediately.

Which of the following correctly describes the water in beaker X?

Temperature of waterAmount of heat in water
(1)increasedincreased
(2)increasedremained the same
(3)remained the sameincreased
(4)remained the sameremained the same

Answer

Option 3. Both lots of water are at the same temperature (50°C), so mixing them does not change the temperature – it stays at 50°C. However, there is now more water (300 ml instead of 200 ml), and a larger amount of water at the same temperature contains more heat. So the temperature stayed the same but the amount of heat increased.

Question 15

2 marks

Substance P is a solid at 30 °C and a liquid at 100 °C.

Which of the following is possible?

Melting point of P (°C)Boiling point of P (°C)
(1)20110
(2)2090
(3)50110
(4)5090

Answer

Option 3. P is solid at 30°C, so it has not yet melted – its melting point must be above 30°C (50°C fits). P is liquid at 100°C, so it has melted but not boiled – its boiling point must be above 100°C (110°C fits). Only option 3 has a melting point above 30°C and a boiling point above 100°C.

Question 16

2 marks

Mary made two puddles on the floor as shown below with the same volume of liquids Y and Z.

The diagram below shows the puddles after an hour.

Which of the following can Mary conclude from her experiment?

  1. The size of the puddle affects the rate of evaporation.
  2. The liquids from puddle Y and puddle Z evaporated at the same rate.
  3. The liquid from puddle Y evaporated at a faster rate than the liquid from puddle Z.
  4. The greater the surface area of the puddle, the faster the rate of evaporation.

Answer

Option 3. The two puddles started with the same volume, but after an hour puddle Y has shrunk much more than puddle Z. So more liquid evaporated from Y in the same time, meaning the liquid from puddle Y evaporated at a faster rate than the liquid from puddle Z. The experiment compares two different liquids (not surface area), so it cannot support a conclusion about surface area.

Booklet B — Open-ended (Questions 17–24, 28 marks)

Question 17

3 marks

Sean wanted to find out about conditions that affect the breeding of mosquitoes.

He conducted an experiment and recorded the number of mosquito eggs that hatched in a day out of 30 eggs at different temperatures. He repeated the experiment two more times. His results are as shown.

Temperature (°C)1st experiment2nd experiment3rd experiment
21151612
27282629
35624
(a) Based on his results, what can Sean conclude about the effect of temperature on the number of mosquito eggs hatched? [2]
(b) Sean sprayed some oil on stagnant water in a well as shown below. Explain how this reduces the breeding of mosquitoes. [1]

Answer

(a) As the temperature increased from 21°C to 27°C, the number of mosquito eggs that hatched increased. As the temperature increased further from 27°C to 35°C, the number of eggs that hatched decreased. (The eggs hatch best at about 27°C.)
(b) The layer of oil floats on the water surface and prevents the mosquito larvae (which live in the water) from taking in air/oxygen at the surface, so the larvae die and fewer mosquitoes are bred.

Question 18

4 marks

Diagram 1 shows the fruit of plant E and its seeds. Diagram 2 shows plant E and its surrounding area. The seeds dispersed from plant E may fall in any of the two areas, A and B.

(a) State the dispersal method for the fruits of plant E. [1]
(b) An equal number of seeds fall in areas A and B. However, there are fewer young plants E growing in area A than in area B. Explain why this is so. [1]
(c) The seed of plant E has a fleshy and sweet-tasting part attached to it as shown below.

Ants will often carry the seeds of plant E back to their underground nest, which is a distance away from plant E. After feeding on the sweet-tasting part on the seed, the ant will move the seed to the waste pile in the nest. Ant droppings can be found in the waste pile.

Explain how these actions of the ants help in the reproduction of plant E. [2]

Answer

(a) By explosive action (the splitting/bursting of the fruit flings the seeds out).
(b) Area A is close to the parent plant E, so the young plants there have to compete more with the parent plant (and one another) for space, sunlight, water and nutrients. Fewer of them survive, so fewer young plants grow in area A than in area B.
(c) The ants carry the seeds far away from the parent plant, so the seeds are dispersed and the new plants do not have to compete with the parent for resources. The ants eat only the sweet-tasting part and leave the seed unharmed, so the seed can still germinate, and the ant droppings in the waste pile provide nutrients for the young seedlings to grow well.

Question 19

4 marks

Animal H is a mammal that lives in a hot place. It has predators that live on land and in water.

(a) During the day, animal H spends most of its time in the water. Suggest a benefit for animal H to stay in the water in the day. [1]
(b) Animal H's nostrils are located near the top of its head as shown below.

Suggest an advantage of the position of the nostrils for the survival of animal H when it is in the water. [1]

(c) Small insects that feed on blood are usually found on the bodies of large mammals. Bird P preys on these small insects. Bird P perches on the back of animal H and pecks at it as shown below.
(i) Explain how bird P would benefit from its relationship with animal H. [1]
(ii) Explain how animal H would benefit from its relationship with bird P. [1]

Answer

(a) It is hot during the day, so staying in the water keeps animal H cool and prevents it from overheating.
(b) Because the nostrils are near the top of the head, animal H can keep most of its head and body underwater (hidden from predators) and still raise just the top of its head above the surface to breathe air.
(i) Bird P benefits because it can feed on the small blood-sucking insects on animal H's body, giving it a supply of food.
(ii) Animal H benefits because bird P removes the harmful blood-sucking insects from its body, keeping animal H free of these pests.

Question 20

3 marks

Joanna made the following observations of organisms, V, W, X, Y and Z.

  • Both V and W are plant eaters.
  • X is only the predator of Y.
  • V and W are the prey of Y.
  • Z gets its energy directly from the Sun.
(a) Draw a food web to show the food relationships among all the organisms above. [2]
(b) Organism W, which is brown in colour, frequently rests on a branch as shown below.

How does the appearance of organism W help in its survival? [1]

Answer

(a) Z gets its energy from the Sun, so Z is the producer. V and W are plant eaters, so they feed on Z. Y is the predator of both V and W, and X is the predator of Y. The completed food web is shown below.
(b) Organism W is brown and rests on a brown branch, so it blends in with (is camouflaged against) its surroundings. This makes it hard for its predators to spot it, helping it to survive.

Question 21

3 marks

A beaker containing substance Q was left on a table. After one hour, the shape of substance Q changed as shown below.

(a) What is the state of matter of substance Q at the start of the experiment? [1]
(b) Fahrid used a funnel to fill a bottle with substance Q as shown below. Substance Q dripped slowly into the bottle at first.

When he lifted the funnel slightly above the mouth of the bottle, he observed that substance Q flowed into the bottle at a faster rate. Explain his observation. [2]

Answer

(a) Liquid. (Substance Q kept the same volume but changed its shape to fit the tilted beaker, which is a property of a liquid.)
(b) When the funnel sits inside the mouth of the bottle, it seals the opening, so the air already inside the bottle cannot escape and it blocks the liquid from entering. When the funnel is lifted slightly above the mouth, there is now a gap so the air in the bottle can escape out through the mouth. This allows substance Q to flow in faster to occupy the space that was previously taken up by the air.

Question 22

3 marks

Jacey has two similar plates made of materials X and Y. He shone a lamp on the plate made of material X and observed the shadow formed on the screen.

He then repeated the experiment with the plate made of material Y. The diagram below shows the shadows formed on the screen.

(a) Based on his observations, state a property of materials X and Y. [1]

Material X: ____________    Material Y: ____________

(b) Jacey set up another experiment as shown. The light sensor on the screen gave a reading of 30 units.

As Jacey moved an object along the surface of the table, the reading on the light sensor remained at 30 units and then dropped to 0 units suddenly.

(i) Which object (lamp, wooden plate or screen) did Jacey move and in which direction? [1]
(ii) Suggest why the reading on the light sensor dropped to 0 units. [1]

Answer

(a) Material X allows some light to pass through it (it is translucent), as it casts a lighter, fainter shadow. Material Y does not allow light to pass through it (it is opaque), as it casts a dark shadow.
(i) Jacey moved the wooden plate towards the lamp. (As it moved nearer the lamp, the plate eventually came into the path of light to the sensor.)
(ii) The reading dropped to 0 units because the wooden plate (which is opaque) blocked all the light from the lamp from reaching the light sensor, so no light fell on the sensor.

Question 23

4 marks

Joy took out a bottle of milk from the refrigerator and placed it on a table. A few minutes later, she observed that there were water droplets on the bottle of milk as shown in the diagram below.

(a) Explain how the water droplets were formed. [2]
(b) Joy placed another bottle of milk from the freezer onto the table. After a short time, a thin layer of white solid was formed on the surface of the bottle.
(i) Explain how the white solid was formed. [1]
(ii) The white solid cannot be seen after a while. Explain why. [1]

Answer

(a) The warm water vapour in the surrounding air came into contact with the colder outer surface of the milk bottle. The water vapour lost heat to the cold bottle, cooled down and condensed into tiny water droplets on the surface of the bottle.
(i) The bottle from the freezer is very cold (below 0°C). The water droplets that condensed on its surface lost heat to the cold bottle and froze, forming a thin layer of white solid (ice/frost).
(ii) As time passes, the ice gains heat from the warmer surroundings, so it melts back into water (which later evaporates). The white solid therefore can no longer be seen.

Question 24

4 marks

Yimei conducted an experiment to find out which material, P or Q, is a better conductor of heat. She poured the same volume of hot water at 90 °C into two similar cups, made of materials P and Q.

Yimei placed the cups on a table in the classroom and measured the temperatures of the water in the cups at different times. Her results for the cup made of material P is shown below.

(a) Explain why the temperature of the water in the cup made of material P decreased with time. [1]
(b) Based on her results, Yimei concluded that material Q is a better conductor of heat than material P. Using a pencil, draw a line on the graph above to show how the temperature of the water in the cup made of material Q changed with time. [1]
(c) Give a reason how each of the following actions helps make her experiment a fair test. [2]

(i) placing the cups at the same place
(ii) using cups of the same size and shape

Answer

(a) The water in cup P was hotter than its surroundings, so it lost heat to the cooler surroundings. As it lost heat its temperature decreased, until it reached the temperature of the surroundings (about 30°C) and stopped falling.
(b) Material Q is a better conductor of heat, so the water in cup Q loses heat faster. Its line must fall more steeply and reach the surrounding temperature (30°C) sooner than cup P, as drawn in red below.
(c) (i) Placing the cups at the same place ensures that the temperature of the surroundings is the same for both cups, so the surroundings do not unfairly affect the cooling of one cup more than the other.
(c) (ii) Using cups of the same size and shape ensures that the exposed surface area of the water/cup is the same for both cups, so heat is lost at a fair rate and only the material being tested differs.
